summary: SkiaSharp rendering performance on macOS is dramatically slower than equivalent native Skia C++ code (sub-10 fps vs 120 fps), with investigation PR #3418 showing that default OpenGL pixel format attributes (4x MSAA, VSync, legacy profile) and rendering loop overhead account for much of the gap.

stepsToReproduce: Run the C++ native Skia benchmark — observes ~120 fps
Run the SkiaSharp managed equivalent — observes <10 fps default
With OpenGL flag optimizations from PR #3418, achieves ~40 fps

repoLinks.description: Original benchmark repo comparing native Skia vs SkiaSharp
Fork with downgraded Skia matching SkiaSharp version (111-119 fps native)
Investigation PR with OpenGL optimizations (Dev/perf testing)

bugSignals.errorMessage: Sub-10 fps default rendering vs 120 fps native C++; ~40 fps with optimized OpenGL flags

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: The SKGLView pixel format attributes and rendering loop have not changed since 3.116.0. PR #3418 is still open and unmerged.

analysis.summary: Significant rendering performance gap between SkiaSharp and native Skia C++ on macOS. The default SKGLView OpenGL configuration uses 4x MSAA, VSync, legacy GL profile, and 24-bit depth buffer — all unnecessary overhead for 2D rendering. PR #3418 demonstrates that removing MSAA, disabling VSync, using GL Core 3.2 profile, and streamlining the render loop improves from <10 fps to ~40 fps, but a gap to native 120 fps remains, suggesting additional overhead in the managed rendering pipeline.
analysis.rationale: Filed by the maintainer with a complete benchmark repo and investigation PR. The performance difference is measurable and reproducible. This is area/SkiaSharp.Views because the bottleneck is in SKGLView's OpenGL configuration and rendering loop, not in the core SkiaSharp bindings. Classified as type/bug because equivalent rendering workloads should perform comparably.

keySignals.text: The native code is 120fps, but skiasharp is sub 10fps
keySignals.interpretation: Order-of-magnitude performance gap confirms real overhead in managed rendering path.

keySignals.text: By first playing around found that I got to 40fps by using the new opengl flags in the SKGLView
keySignals.interpretation: OpenGL pixel format configuration is a major contributor — 4x improvement from flag changes alone.

keySignals.text: My fork which downgrades skia to the same version in skiasharp is a tiny bit slower, 111-119 fps
keySignals.interpretation: Skia version difference accounts for only ~1-9 fps — the majority of the gap is SkiaSharp overhead.

keySignals.text: This all appears to be inside the skia code, which appears to be using similar compiler args
keySignals.interpretation: Compiler optimization flags are not the culprit — the gap is in the GL setup and rendering pipeline.

codeInvestigation.finding: Default pixel format attributes request 4x MSAA (Multisample + SampleBuffers=1 + Samples=4), DoubleBuffer, 24-bit depth, and 32-bit color. PR #3418 shows removing MSAA and reducing depth to 0 significantly improves performance.

DrawRect() includes backing scale factor tracking with deferred redraw, renderTarget validity check, SKAutoCanvasRestore, and triple flush (canvas.Flush + context.Flush + OpenGLContext.FlushBuffer). PR #3418 replaces this with a streamlined RenderDirect() and RenderFrame() pair that bypasses scale deferral and extra checks.

GRContext.Flush() calls either gr_direct_context_flush_and_submit or gr_direct_context_flush depending on the submit parameter. The default DrawRect calls both canvas.Flush() (which calls GRContext.Flush) and context.Flush() — potentially double-flushing.
SKCanvas.Flush() delegates to (Context as GRContext)?.Flush() — meaning canvas.Flush() followed by context.Flush() in SKGLView.DrawRect is redundant double-flush to the GPU pipeline.

GRContextOptions exposes limited tuning knobs (AvoidStencilBuffers, RuntimeProgramCacheSize, GlyphCacheTextureMaximumBytes, AllowPathMaskCaching, DoManualMipmapping, BufferMapThreshold). No options for controlling shader compilation strategy or GPU pipeline fencing.

nextQuestions: After GL config optimizations (~40 fps), what accounts for the remaining 3x gap to native 120 fps?
Is the remaining overhead from P/Invoke transition cost, managed memory allocation, or GPU pipeline stalls?
Do other platforms (iOS Metal, Windows Direct3D) show a similar performance gap?
Would a CVDisplayLink-based render loop (bypassing NSView.DrawRect entirely) close the gap further?

resolution.hypothesis: The performance gap has multiple causes: (1) default 4x MSAA in pixel format, (2) VSync capping framerate, (3) legacy GL profile, (4) redundant double-flush in rendering loop, (5) overhead from NSView.DrawRect event dispatch vs tight native loop. PR #3418 addresses (1)-(3) and partially (5), achieving ~40 fps — the remaining gap likely requires deeper pipeline optimization.

proposals.title: Optimize default OpenGL pixel format attributes
proposals.description: Remove default 4x MSAA, use GL Core 3.2 profile, reduce depth buffer to 0 — matches changes in PR #3418. This alone yields a 4x improvement (<10 fps → ~40 fps) with no API changes.

proposals.title: Expose VSync control on SKGLView
proposals.description: Add a SwapInterval property (or VSync boolean) to SKGLView so users can disable VSync for benchmarking or uncapped rendering. PR #3418 sets SwapInterval=0 in PrepareOpenGL.

proposals.title: Fix redundant double-flush in DrawRect
proposals.description: DrawRect calls canvas.Flush() then context.Flush(). Since canvas.Flush() already calls context.Flush(), the second call is redundant and forces an extra GPU pipeline sync. Remove the canvas.Flush() call.

proposals.title: Add RenderDirect() for tight rendering loops
proposals.description: Expose a public RenderDirect() method that bypasses the NSView.DrawRect dispatch overhead, as prototyped in PR #3418. This allows callers to drive the render loop directly for maximum throughput.

resolution.recommendedProposal: Optimize default OpenGL pixel format attributes
resolution.recommendedReason: Provides the largest single improvement (~4x) with the smallest change. The MSAA and legacy profile defaults are unnecessarily expensive for 2D rendering and match no practical use case.

actionability.reason: Active investigation by maintainer with open PR #3418. Performance gap is confirmed and reproducible, but root cause of remaining 3x gap after GL optimizations needs profiling.

actions.description: Apply performance bug labels for macOS OpenGL rendering
Cross-reference with Blazor WASM rendering performance #3313
